params.text:
Why older and frail people fall

Falls among the people we care for are rarely down to a single cause. NICE guidance (CG161, now updated as NG249) describes falls as multifactorial – several risk factors usually combine. Recognising them helps you reduce the risk for each individual.

Crucially, a history of falling in the past year is the strongest single predictor that someone may fall again, so always take previous falls seriously.

params.title: Tap a card to flip it
params.description: Common reasons older or frail people are at higher risk of falling.

dialogs.answer: Muscle weakness, stiff joints and unsteadiness make trips and stumbles more likely and harder to recover from.
Some medicines, and taking several at once, can cause drowsiness, dizziness or low blood pressure that leads to a fall. Sudden changes in medication warrant extra care.
Poor vision, the wrong glasses or dim lighting make hazards hard to judge, especially on stairs and unfamiliar routes.
Rushing to reach the toilet, particularly at night, is a very common trigger for falls. Easy access and prompt support reduce the risk.
Dementia, delirium or confusion can affect judgement of hazards and awareness of one's own limitations.
Ill-fitting slippers, loose rugs, clutter and trailing flexes in the person's own home all add to the risk.
